story:
When I was fifteen, Mom announced we were going to her great-aunt Mildred’s funeral. I’d never heard of her. Dad translated “pay homage” as “get in the car,” and we drove six sweltering July hours to Paducah, Kentucky, in a new Ford with no air conditioning and all the windows down—except Mom’s, because the wind “messed up” her hair.

The Paducah Southern Old-Time Christian Church was a low, airless building that seated about 125. I detoured to a restroom—an in-house outhouse—and when I stepped back into the sanctuary, July slapped me again. Inside, the congregation practiced the ancient art of paper-fan survival.

A murmur rose as the Reverend James A. Pastoric appeared, Bible in one hand, sweat towel on his shoulder, and a mountainous crown of red hair that made him look like a walking Olympic torch. He had nothing for Sister Mildred, he said—she’d gone on to her reward—but plenty for “you sinners,” who, he warned, were headed for hell unless we repented. For an hour he thundered fire and brimstone, blotting his face each time he said “brimstone,” which I learned must cause industrial-grade sweating.

Finally paroled, we were funneled past the open casket. Great-Aunt Mildred lay frosty pale in a matching dress, her face ghost white except for silver-dollar puffs of rouge that made each cheek look like the Japanese flag.

We joined the caravan to the cemetery. After snaking past creeks, tracks, and fields, we stopped at the bottom of a hill pitched like a ski jump. “Not a job for overweight middle-aged beer drinkers,” Dad observed. The first team of young pallbearers heaved the casket uphill, legs wobbling on the uneven slope; every misstep pitched the load at a new angle. After a hundred yards they set it down for the second team. Before the subs got control, the casket began to slide backward—only a quick grab kept it from becoming a sled and turning the elders into bowling pins.

At last the casket reached the crest and was set beside the grave. Gravediggers waited with professional solemnity. The mourners formed a semicircle. “Would anybody like to see Sister Mildred for the last time?” the pastor asked. “Yes, please,” her oldest daughter said. “For God’s sake,” Mom whispered.

He opened the lid. There was Mildred, somehow on her side, head cocked toward us and one eye popped open. The rouge had melted, trailing into the corner of her mouth. The front edge of her upper plate had come loose, exposing a glimpse of tongue streaked red—more horror film than hymn. A collective gasp. Then, balanced as she was, Mildred pitched, and her left arm flopped out of the casket. Screams: “She’s alive!”

The undertaker lunged, tucked the arm back, and shut the lid. Silence. No prayer, no benediction. A handful of mourners turned and fled; the rest followed down the hill in a stunned, amoeba-shaped retreat.

We reached the car without a word. Then Mom giggled, Dad snorted, and I broke. We laughed all the way home; my stomach hurt for three days. Even now, remembering that day makes me smile—and that smile is how I pay homage to Great-Aunt Mildred. I’ll admit the funeral happened, but the calamity here is gleefully exaggerated; that’s part of the tale.
